description: End-to-end agent loop: an AI coding agent picks up a Jira ticket, uses Jira as execution context, implements it, and opens a PR linked back to the ticket. The coding and PR-creation themselves are outside Jira — same cost both arms, omitted here. Steps 0-2 use measured per-op benchmarks; steps 3-6 use numbers derived from the committed fixtures (no Jira mutations performed).
scenario: Agent receives: 'Work on BENCH-1 and open a PR linked to it.' The Jira interactions that make up the loop:

steps.label: List related assigned tickets (context for blockers)
Transition To Do -> In Progress
steps.source: derived: skill = short `acli transition` cmd (~17 tok) + one-line ack (~13 tok); mcp = tool-call JSON (~33 tok) + status-block response inferred from fixture shape (~100 tok)

steps.label: Comment: 'starting work'
steps.source: derived: skill = acli heredoc cmd (~33 tok) + ack w/ URL (~20 tok); mcp = tool-call JSON (~50 tok) + full comment object (662 tok) extracted from fields.comment.comments[0] in fixtures/mcp-barebones.json

notes: Steps 3-6 are derived from committed fixtures and `acli`/MCP tool-call shapes — not live-captured. No Jira mutations were performed.

MCP's addCommentToJiraIssue response is dominated by the returned comment object (self URL, author + updateAuthor user objects with timeZone, created/updated timestamps, body). Its size is taken from the embedded comment[0] in the barebones fixture — same shape, sanitize is size-preserving.

transitionJiraIssue response shape isn't captured directly; assumed to be a status-block plus brief envelope (~400 chars, ~100 tok). If MCP actually returns the full updated issue, this estimate is conservative by ~1500 tok per transition.
Reasoning tokens between tool calls are not counted; they scale roughly with tool-result size, so the true end-to-end gap is likely a bit larger.
